1: A type: YHWH initiates the cities of refuge — their establishment is a divine command, not a human invention. Christ as the refuge of the soul was not a human religious concept; he was appointed by the Father: "God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement" (Romans 3:25). The cities of refuge are a divine institution of grace, pointing to the one who is graciously appointed as shelter from judgment.
2: A type: "Tell the Israelites: Set apart for yourselves the cities of refuge that I commanded through Moses." The cities are "set apart" (qadesh, the root of holiness/consecration) for a specific purpose — asylum. Christ is the "holy, blameless, pure, set apart from sinners" (Hebrews 7:26) high priest who is himself the city of refuge. The set-apart nature of the cities points to the holy one who is the asylum for sinners.
3: A type with direct NT application: "anyone who kills a person accidentally or unintentionally may flee there for sanctuary from the blood avenger." Hebrews 6:18 uses the city-of-refuge language directly: "we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us may be greatly encouraged... we have this hope as an anchor for the soul." The "fleeing" to Christ for refuge from judgment is the conscious NT application of the city-of-refuge type. The blood avenger (gōʾēl haddām) who pursues the manslayer corresponds to the condemning function of the law and the accuser.
4: A type: "When the person flees to one of these cities and stops at the city gate, he must present his case to the elders of that city. They are to admit him." The city gate as the place of legal judgment — the elders who receive and hear the case — points to Christ as the one who stands at the gate: "I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved" (John 10:9). The accused does not approach a distant deity but presents himself at the accessible gate, where he is received by those authorized to grant asylum.
5: A type: "If the blood avenger pursues him, they must not surrender the accused, because he struck the person unintentionally and without prior malice." Christ's intercession ensures that the accusation cannot prevail against those who are in him: "Who will bring any charge against those whom God has chosen? It is God who justifies. Who then is the one who condemns? No one. Christ Jesus who died — more than that, who was raised to life — is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us" (Romans 8:33-34). The city of refuge will not surrender its resident to the blood avenger; Christ will not surrender his people to the accuser.
6: A direct type with NT fulfillment: "He is to stay in that city until he has stood trial before the congregation, and until the death of the high priest who is serving at that time. After that, the manslayer may return." The death of the high priest releases the manslayer from confinement — a permanent amnesty through the priest's death. Hebrews 9:15 is the direct fulfillment: "Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance — now that he has died as a ransom to set them free from the sins committed under the first covenant." Christ's death as high priest releases all who were under the confinement of the law's condemnation. The manslayer's release is the shadow; the believer's justification through the death of the great High Priest is the substance.
7: A type: three cities of refuge on the west side of the Jordan: Kedesh (in Naphtali/Galilee), Shechem (in Ephraim/central), Hebron (in Judah/south). They are geographically distributed so that no one in any part of the western land is more than a day's journey from asylum. Christ is the asylum who is universally accessible: "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved" (Romans 10:13). No region, no distance, no background excludes a person from access to the refuge.
8: A type: three more cities on the east side of the Jordan: Bezer (Reuben), Ramoth-gilead (Gad), Golan (Manasseh). The six cities together — three west, three east — cover the entire inhabited land of Israel. The geographic completeness of the refuge system points to the universal availability of Christ: "there is no difference between Jew and Gentile — the same Lord is Lord of all and richly blesses all who call on him" (Romans 10:12). No one is geographically or ethnically excluded from access to the refuge.
9: A type: "These were the designated cities for all the Israelites and for the foreigners living among them." The cities of refuge are explicitly extended to the gēr — the sojourner, the non-Israelite resident. This inclusion of foreigners is the type of Christ's universal offer: "there is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). The asylum from judgment is offered to insiders and outsiders alike — anyone who accidentally killed someone, anyone who stands under the condemnation of the law, may flee to the refuge and find safety until the death of the High Priest sets them free.
